steps.title: Track Vale's hidden power
steps.content: The passage says people in far-off provinces may not talk daily about Henry Vale, but they read The Jupiter and treat it as essential. Vale's glory is described as hidden but still conscious, which means his satisfaction does not depend on open fame. He enjoys knowing about his influence even when others are not loudly praising him.

steps.title: Use the club scene
steps.content: The club scene reinforces that private satisfaction. Vale sits silently while politicians chatter, thinking that they are all in his power. He imagines he could strike at the loudest of them if it were worth raising his pen. The detail connects his newspaper writing with the ability to shape or damage public figures.

steps.title: Reject unsupported claims
steps.content: Choice A invents a comparison between readers' interest in Vale and their interest in politicians. Choice B invents public praise and influential people's respect for him. Choice C misreads hidden satisfaction as a desire for distant admiration. Choice D captures his private satisfaction in his influence. The answer is D.
